revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:中世日本紀]][[ko:중세 일본기]]
{{Citation method|date=December 2021|qq=}} '''Chūsei Nihonki''' (中世日本紀) is a scholarly term used in medieval Japan to refer either to the collective term for a group of myths that were variously interpreted and reorganized—primarily in accordance with the {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|qid=Q3080781|lt=Honchi Suijaku|1=Honji suijaku}} theory while also drawing on works such as the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}—or to the movement of such reinterpretations and reorganizations. The former is also called '''Medieval Mythology'''. In Medieval Nihonki, the gods of the {{ill|Kiki|ja|記紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kiki (Shinto)|qid=Q842308}} are often identified with the various deities and Buddhas of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, and myths are recounted in which kami and buddhas contend on equal footing on the same stage, reflecting the syncretic ideas of {{ill|Ryōbu Shintō|ja|両部神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryōbu Shintō|1=Ryobu Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4402813}} and {{ill|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|en|Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō|qid=Q4407806|lt=Sannō Shintō|1=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} prevalent in medieval times. Furthermore, even where such identifications do not occur, interpretations of myths influenced by Buddhism can be seen. Since these accounts are primarily recorded in texts such as {{ill|Utakagaku|ja|歌学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utakagaku|1=kagaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11545105}} literature, military chronicles ({{ill|Gunki Monogatari|ja|軍記物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunki Monogatari|1=gunkimono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11637452}}), and temple and shrine legends, there exists no unified or systematic corpus, and a wealth of variations remains. == Background == Medieval society witnessed Buddhism permeating every corner of life, and while remnants of magical beliefs persisted, there was also a demand for rational and abstract thought. In the era of the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制#日本の律令制|lt=Ritsuryō state|lt_ja=律令国家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q742460}}, the interpretation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}—initially undertaken mainly by court historians, scholars from the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|qid=Q3012048|lt=University|1=Daigaku-ryō}} ({{ill|Doctorate|en|Doctorate|qid=Q849697|lt=Doctor|1=Doctorate}}), and ritual clans ({{ill|Clan|en|Clan|qid=Q211503|lt=Clan|1=Clan}}) responsible for palace ceremonies—gradually shifted under the dynastic state ({{ill|Dynastic State|ja|王朝国家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dynastic State|1=ōchō kokka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11573109}}) to practitioners of esoteric Buddhism, monks ({{ill|Sangha|en|Sangha|qid=Q193944|lt=Monk|1=Sangha}}), Utakagaku scholars, and shrine priests ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjin|1=Jinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11588676}}), thereby prompting a reinterpretation of myths based on Buddhist thought. == Major Myths == === Ebisu Worship === According to the {{ill|Taiheiki|en|Taiheiki|qid=Q1152189|lt=Taiheiki|1=Taiheiki}}, it is said that {{ill|Hiruko|ja|ヒルコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1067028}} is {{ill|Nishinomiya Shrine|en|Nishinomiya Shrine|qid=Q705297|lt=Nishinomiya Daimyōjin|1=Nishinomiya Shrine}}. This theory is attributed to the {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|卜部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}}’s {{ill|Urabe Kan'in|ja|卜部兼員|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe Kan'in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who for generations made the interpretation of the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} a family enterprise. Furthermore, in works such as {{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|qid=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike|1=The Tale of the Heike}}, {{ill|Genpei Jōsuiki|en|Genpei Jōsuiki|qid=Q3020148|lt=Genpei Seisuiki|1=Genpei Jōsuiki}}, {{ill|Kokin Wakashū Jo Monogatari Sanryūshō|ja|古今和歌集序聞書三流抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokin Wakashū Jo Monogatari Sanryūshō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Shintōshū|en|Shintōshū|qid=Q2616931|lt=Shintōshu|1=Shintōshū}}, a widely recounted theory identifies Hiruko with {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}, narrating that he drifted to {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|qid=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}} and became known as “Yosaburō-dono” after being granted Nishinomiya by {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}. === Ame-no-Murakumo Sword === An anecdote in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} (in the annals of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Tenji Tennō|1=Emperor Tenji}}; see also the {{ill|Kusanagi Sword Theft Incident|ja|草薙剣盗難事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi Sword Theft Incident|1=theft of Kusanagi no Tsurugi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q861812}}) recounts that the Silla monk {{ill|Michiyuki (disambiguation)|en|Michiyuki (disambiguation)|qid=Q11195644|lt=Dōgyō|1=Michiyuki (disambiguation)}} attempted to steal the divine sword, {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|en|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|qid=Q14944|lt=Ame-no-Murakumo Sword|1=Kusanagi no Tsurugi}}, from {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|en|Atsuta Shrine|qid=Q482065|lt=Atsuta Shrine|1=Atsuta Shrine}} and bring it back to Silla. However, in {{ill|Ōe no Masafusa|en|Ōe no Masafusa|qid=Q3135978|lt=Ōe no Masafusa|1=Ōe no Masafusa}}’s {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine Chronicle|ja|筥崎宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakozaki Shrine Chronicle|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, when the Ame-no-Murakumo Sword—which had repeatedly eluded Dōgyō—could no longer escape, {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|qid=Q261637|lt=Usa Hachiman|1=Hachiman}}, the manifestation of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|qid=Q236242|lt=Amitābha|1=Amitābha}}, is said to have struck Dōgyō down and seized the sword (known as the Kusanagi Sword). Furthermore, in {{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|qid=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike|1=The Tale of the Heike}} and {{ill|Gukanshō|en|Gukanshō|qid=Q1368208|lt=Gukan Shō|1=Gukanshō}}, it is recounted that {{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|qid=Q316651|lt=Emperor Antoku|1=Emperor Antoku}} was the reincarnation of {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|qid=Q1054437|lt=Yamata no Orochi|1=Yamata no Orochi}}, and that he recovered his treasured sword—previously seized by Orochi—and returned it to the Dragon Palace. In response, the {{ill|Taiheiki|en|Taiheiki|qid=Q1152189|lt=Taiheiki|1=Taiheiki}} states that following the {{ill|Jōkyū War|en|Jōkyū War|qid=Q1372308|lt=Jōkyū War|1=Jōkyū War}}, the rise of the warrior class and the decline of imperial prestige were due to the treasured sword having sunk to the bottom of the sea, prompting {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} to issue a divine edict to the Dragon Palace to cast the sword up onto the Ise coast. === The Sixth Heavenly Demon King and Amaterasu === In the {{ill|Shasekishū|en|Shasekishū|qid=Q2709914|lt=Sasekishu|1=Shasekishū}}, there is a passage recorded as having been relayed by the priests of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, which reads as follows. When {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} created the land of Japan, she noticed an imprint of {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|1=Vairocana}} within the sea and probed the seabed with her spear, from which droplets began to fall. Observing this, the {{ill|Sixth Heavenly Demon King|ja|天魔|1=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|lt=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|1=Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|qq=Sixth Heavenly Demon King|lt_ja=第六天魔王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixth Heavenly Demon King|13=User:Immanuelle/Demon King of the Sixth Heaven|qq=}} proclaimed, "These droplets will form a nation that will disseminate the Buddhist law and manifest the order of human life and death," and sought to destroy the country. In response, Amaterasu retorted, "I shall not utter the name of the {{ill|Three Treasures|ja|三宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Treasures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Three Jewels|qid=Q207957}} nor permit you to approach me; begone," and drove him away. To uphold this promise, Ise Shrine refrains from allowing monks to approach and renders Buddhist terminology in {{ill|euphemism|ja|隠語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/euphemism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Argot|qid=Q3462602}}, though in truth it steadfastly safeguards the Three Treasures. === Toyouke Ōmikami === Within {{ill|Ise faith|lt=Ise faith|lt=Ise Shintō|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shintō|qq=}}, the priests of the outer shrine of Ise—namely, the {{ill|Togai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togai clan|1=Watarai clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11486123}}—elevated the enshrined deity of the outer shrine, {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}}, to a status equal to or higher than that of the inner shrine’s deity ({{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}). To achieve this, they identified Toyouke-Omikami with the deities that appeared prior to the creation of the heavens and the earth ({{ill|Japanese creation myth|en|Japanese creation myth|qid=Q6158445|lt=Creation|1=Japanese creation myth}}), namely {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|en|Ame-no-Minakanushi|qid=Q2842779|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi|1=Ame-no-Minakanushi}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|en|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|qid=Q478504|lt=Kunikotomotachi-no-Kami|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, thereby establishing a universal divine status that surpassed Amaterasu, and various traditions were developed. Additionally, from the original intent of constructing the grand outer shrine to enshrine the food deity, some have interpreted this as, quite literally, a restoration movement for {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}}, whose importance to the imperial family—despite being highly venerated—was at times seen as a potential challenge to the exclusive sanctity of the imperial institution. == Academic Research == Since {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}’s {{ill|Kojiki-den|en|Kojiki-den|qid=Q11410835|lt=Kojikiden|1=Kojiki-den}}, myth interpretation has moved in the direction of discarding the Confucian and Buddhist "discordant sentiments," eventually leading to modern {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shintō|1=State Shinto}}. In particular, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} proclaimed during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}} dealt a devastating blow to the syncretic ideology of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu Shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, and under the doctrine of {{ill|Secular Shrine Theory|en|Secular Shrine Theory|qid=Q106651561|lt=Non-religious Shrine Theory|1=Secular Shrine Theory}}, Shintō teachings shed their overt religious aspects in favor of serving as a basis for national customs, rituals, and spiritual support. Moreover, medieval mythology was seldom revisited in modern times, being dismissed as absurd folklore. In the 1960s, {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} pioneered research on "medieval mythology," and studies in fields such as {{ill|mythology|ja|神話学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Mythology|qid=Q9134}} and {{ill|history|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/history|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1066186}} advanced alongside those from the perspective of Japanese literature. In literature, medieval mythology was reinterpreted as an intellectual experiment aimed at achieving metaphysical thought on a par with Buddhism by utilizing traditional mythological systems, while in historical studies it was reappraised as a method by which the dynastic state reorganized the spiritual world of warriors and others imbued with Buddhist rationality. Furthermore, {{ill|Uejima Kyō|ja|上島享|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uejima Kyō|1=Susumu Uejima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24576799}} has pointed out that one factor leading to the emergence of Medieval Nihonki was the crisis of "dynastic change" recognized during Taira no Masakado’s revolt, which necessitated an emphasis on the emperor’s long history and unbroken lineage in order to maintain and reinforce imperial legitimacy. He further argues that the {{ill|Divine Nation Concept|ja|神国観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Divine Nation Concept|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神国|1=Shinkoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5367717}}—which stressed that the emperor and Amaterasu governed Japan and its people—and the {{ill|Tripartite View|ja|三国観|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tripartite View|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which posited that Japan, China, and India coexisted (with Buddhism flourishing in Japan, a land originally considered peripheral), spread not only among intellectuals but also among the general populace, thereby serving as both a theoretical pillar supporting medieval royal power and a robust popular foundation that has underpinned the imperial image and national character from early modern times to the present. == Footnotes == <references/> == References == * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'The Transformation of the Gods: From the World of Shrine and Temple Legends' ({{ill|University of Tokyo Press|en|University of Tokyo Press|qid=Q842460|lt=University of Tokyo Press|1=University of Tokyo Press}} 1976, revised edition 2009, ISBN 4130230174 / {{ill|Chikuma Gakugei Bunko|ja|ちくま学芸文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma Gakugei Bunko|1=Chikuma Gakugei Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11271732}}, March 2000) * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'The Formation of Medieval Japanese Culture: Myth and Historical Narration' (University of Tokyo Press, April 1981) * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'The Appearance of the Recluse: The Hermit's Life and Spirit' (Hanawa Shobo <Hanawa Sensho>, 1967) * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'The Hermits of Japan' (Hanawa Shobo <Hanawa Shinsho>, 1969, revised edition 1986 / On-demand edition 2005) * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'The Thoughts and Expressions of Medieval Japanese' ({{ill|Mirai-sha|ja|未来社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mirai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 未來社|1=Miraisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11519454}}, 1970) * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'The Spiritual Historical Landscape of Medieval Japan' (Hanawa Shobo, 1974, revised edition 1986) * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'The Mist of Yoshino: Taiheiki' ({{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|qid=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha|1=Heibonsha}} Meisaku Bunko, July 1978 / {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4346755}} "Revisiting Japanese History", 2016) * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'Coming from the Heavens: For Medieval Discourse' ({{ill|Jinbun Shoin|ja|人文書院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinbun Shoin|1=Jimbun Shoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11376034}}, June 1983) * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'Royal Power, Religion, and the Arts in Medieval Japan' (Jinbun Shoin, October 1988) * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'Rituals and Annotations: Ancient Myths in Medieval Times' ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4346755}}, September 1993) * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'The Dismantling of the Ritual State: Essays on Medieval Cultural History' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, June 1996) * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'Myths and Historical Narratives of Medieval Japan' ({{ill|Iwata Shoin|ja|岩田書院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwata Shoin|1=Iwata Shoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109596820}}, October 2006) * {{ill|Sakurai Kōrō|ja|桜井好朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai Kōrō|1=Yoshirō Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11537795}} 'Gods and Buddhas: The World of Buddhist Reception and Shinbutsu Shūgō' ({{ill|Shunjusha|ja|春秋社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shunjusha|1=Shunjusha Publishing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11513793}} [The Great Series on Buddhism and the Japanese], 1985, revised edition 2000) * {{ill|Saitō Eiki|ja|斎藤英喜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitō Eiki|1=Hideki Saitō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11500664}} 'Reinterpreted Japanese Myths' ({{ill|Kodansha Modern Works|ja|講談社現代新書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kodansha Modern Works|1=Kodansha's new library of knowledge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11632223}}, 2006) ISBN 4-06-149871-1 == Related Topics == * {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|qid=Q814158|lt=Japanese Mythology|1=Japanese mythology}} * {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Japanese Buddhism|1=Buddhism in Japan}} * {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|qid=Q3080781|lt=Honchi Suijaku|1=Honji suijaku}} * {{ill|Ryōbu Shintō|ja|両部神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryōbu Shintō|1=Ryobu Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4402813}} * {{ill|Fudoki|en|Fudoki|qid=Q1194534|lt=Fudoki|1=Fudoki}} * {{ill|Koshi Kojiden|ja|古史古伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshi Kojiden|1=koshi koden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11410976}} * {{ill|Yoshida Shintō|en|Yoshida Shintō|qid=Q1760351|lt=Yoshida Shintō|1=Yoshida Shintō}} == External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ Kokugakuin University "Formation of a Hub for National Learning-Based Research Dissemination of Shintō and Japanese Culture"] {{Shintō Navigation|qq=}} {{Medieval Shintō|qq=}} {{Medieval Shinto|qq=}}
[[ja:中世日本紀]]
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
{{translated page|ja|中世日本紀|version=100860386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q11362829}} [[Category:Cultural history of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]]
